label: Churn-save / cancellation reply
prior_art_examples: Churn-save replies are categorically different from general support: they're rare (1-3x/week for a healthy seed-stage SaaS), high-emotional-load, and the founder's voice is load-bearing because aggressive automation backfires. The community consensus across founder-facing playbooks: (1) Baremetrics' 'The 17 emails we send to engage customers, reduce churn & increase revenue' codifies the canonical founder-cancellation email as a short, plain-text message from the founder's address — no marketing template, no images, no 'we value you' filler. Opens with 'Hey [name], I saw you cancelled.' Closes with a single open-ended question: 'Mind telling me what made it not work for you?' (baremetrics.com/blog/lifecycle-emails). (2) Sequenzy's 2026 churn-save playbook explicitly warns against blanket discounting: 'Don't send discounts to everyone who's about to churn. It trains users to threaten cancellation whenever they want a deal. Save discounts for specific situations… Instead, offer: a call with the founder, extended trial of premium features, or help migrating their data/workflow' (sequenzy.com/blog/churn-prevention-email-sequence). (3) Outseta's 'How To Handle Your Start-up's Most Common Objections' goes further: a 'do-not-discount' rule is a deliberate brand-integrity stance for small SaaS — the company's price IS its value claim, and softening it under cancellation pressure trains the market that price is negotiable (outseta.com/posts/handling-startup-objections). (4) Patrick McKenzie ('patio11') in his Bingo Card Creator and Stripe writing has long advocated 'founder personally replies, never offers a discount, asks one question, offers concrete help'. Representative output shape observed across these sources: 4 sentences. Sentence 1: acknowledgement of the cancellation — no apology, no surprise. Sentence 2: a specific reaction grounded in something the customer said (or a generic but specific-feeling fallback). Sentence 3: a concrete non-discount offer (founder call, extended trial, migration help, feature roadmap context, decision-based 'here's why we don't do X'). Sentence 4: a single open question OR a graceful door-open close ('whatever you decide, the door's open'). Crisp MagicReply and Intercom Fin do NOT publicly target this use case — both pitch tier-1 deflection — which is exactly why BBC's support studio has differentiated value here: brain memory makes the founder-personal-reply scalable instead of generic.
prompt_pattern: Same skeleton as customer-reply (role line, voiceClause, product clause, glossary clause, inputs, output instruction) PLUS three churn-specific layers that flip the default behavior: (1) decisionsClause is REQUIRED, not optional. The 'we don't discount under $X' rule, 'we don't do annual prepay deals', 'we don't offer pause for <plan>' rules live in brain.recent_decisions and the prompt is told 'these are non-negotiable. If the customer's cancellation reason would require violating one, acknowledge the constraint honestly and offer the alternative that does not violate it.' This is structurally analogous to engineering's adr-draft pattern — decisions are first-class context, not background. (2) Tone modulation: severity defaults to 'medium' (not 'low') because cancellation is inherently medium-stakes; voice register is kept but cheerfulness is dampened (forbid_pattern equivalents in the prompt: no exclamation points, no 'Thanks for being a customer!', no 'we'd love to keep you'). (3) Constrained offer menu: the prompt enumerates the offer types the founder allows (passed as an optional input + as a structural list in the system prompt). Default menu: ['founder call', 'extended trial', 'migration help', 'roadmap context', 'graceful goodbye with door open']. Discount is conspicuously absent unless the user adds it via override. Output: kind='plain', body is exactly 4 short paragraphs (or 4 sentences if the inbound was short). The prompt enforces a hard ceiling of 6 sentences total — long churn-save replies are read as defensive. Few-shot is more justified here than for customer-reply: the brain may not have enough cancellation-shaped example_phrases, so the template ships with 2-3 hardcoded shape exemplars in the prompt itself (patio11/Baremetrics-style 4-sentence replies) that demonstrate cadence without copying content. These are voice-neutral exemplars (about hypothetical products) used purely for shape, not for tone or content.
failure_modes: 1. Offering a discount when the brain has a 'no-discount' decision recorded — catastrophic for brand integrity and the single highest-stakes failure mode for this template. Mitigated by making decisionsClause REQUIRED + an explicit 'do not violate any prior decision' instruction. 2. Begging tone — the model defaults to retention-marketing register ('we'd hate to see you go', 'is there anything we can do'). Both phrases are forbid_pattern candidates by default. 3. Defensive over-explanation — model writes 3 paragraphs about why the product is good actually. Cancellation replies that argue lose; the prompt enforces 6-sentence hard ceiling. 4. Accepting cancellation reason at face value when the brain's product memory contradicts it — e.g., customer says 'you don't have integration X' but product.differentiators says integration X shipped 2 months ago. Model should gently surface the mismatch ('we actually shipped X in March — happy to walk you through it if it's still relevant') rather than mute-acceptance. 5. Founder-call offer with no calendar grounding — model says 'let's hop on a call' without a link; downstream the founder forgets to add one and the offer dies. The prompt should emit a placeholder ('[calendar link]') the founder pastes in. 6. Citing a decision the customer doesn't have context for — 'as our pricing policy states…' reads cold; the citation tag should still appear (for the founder's review) but the surface text should soften ('we've made a deliberate choice not to discount because…').
brain_memory_consumed: voice (REQUIRED) — same as customer-reply but with extra dampening; example_phrases under-supplies churn-shaped examples so the prompt augments with built-in exemplars. product (REQUIRED) — positioning + differentiators are used to detect when the customer's stated reason contradicts shipped features (failure mode 4 above). glossary (REQUIRED) — same terminology grounding. decision (REQUIRED — primary differentiator from customer-reply) — 'we don't discount under $X', 'no pause for <plan>', 'no annual prepay deals', 'we don't do custom contracts under $Y MRR' all live here. Used both to constrain offers AND cited inline so the founder can review which decision shaped the reply. team (OPTIONAL) — sign-off resolution; for a solo founder this is one row. vendor (rarely consumed) — only when the cancellation mentions a vendor-shaped pain point ('your Stripe billing failed me twice'). source_artifact (OPTIONAL) — if the brain has a public 'pricing philosophy' or 'why we don't discount' blog post recorded as source_artifact, the reply can link to it. skill, note — not consumed.
inputs_required: [{id: 'cancellation_message', label: 'Cancellation message', hint: 'Paste the customer's cancellation email / cancel-flow survey response / chat message. The more verbatim, the better — the AI uses the specific phrasing to mirror back.', required: true, kind: 'text' (textarea)}, {id: 'customer_name', label: 'Customer name', hint: 'First name. Churn-save replies that open 'Hi there' fail.', required: false, kind: 'text'}, {id: 'tenure', label: 'How long have they been a customer? (optional)', hint: 'E.g. '6 months', '2 years', 'just signed up last week'. Changes the register — long-tenure churn is heavier than short-tenure.', required: false, kind: 'text'}, {id: 'plan', label: 'Plan / MRR tier (optional)', hint: 'Hobby / Pro / Team / Enterprise — or actual MRR if you have it. Routes which 'don't discount under X' decision applies.', required: false, kind: 'text'}, {id: 'allowed_offers', label: 'Offers I'm willing to make (optional)', hint: 'Comma-separated. Default: founder-call, extended-trial, migration-help, roadmap-context. Add 'discount' explicitly if you want to permit one this time.', required: false, kind: 'text'}]. The `allowed_offers` input is the explicit, in-band 'override-this-once' lever that complements the persistent J.14/J.15 conversational override flow — it lets the founder relax the no-discount rule for a single high-value account without changing the brain.
output_block_kinds: Same as customer-reply: kind='plain' with markdown body. No new OutputBlock kind warranted — the four-sentence churn-save reply is a strict subset of the markdown the plain block already supports. If a future v1.x adds an `email_draft` kind for outbound emails (e.g., a Founder Studio 'investor-update' template), churn-save could optionally migrate to it for the subject-line field, but in practice churn-save replies live inside the existing thread the customer started with their cancellation email, so subject is fixed.
comparable_template_in_existing_studios: Engineering's `adr-draft` is the closest structural analog — both are 'high-stakes document where prior decisions are first-class context'. The decisionsClause helper from eng-templates/types.ts is reused verbatim. The difference: adr-draft produces a long structured document with required sections; churn-save produces a constrained ≤6-sentence reply with no section structure. The shared DNA is 'decisions are not optional context'. Customer-reply is the closest in shape (inbound→reply, same input pattern); churn-save is customer-reply with decisions promoted to required and tone explicitly dampened.
memory_writeback_proposal: Three writeback paths, all valuable. (1) Cancellation-reason ledger → memory_files row of kind='note' tagged 'support/cancellation-reasons', one row per accepted churn-save run, containing {reason_summary: LLM-extracted one-line, plan, tenure, accepted_at, offer_made, outcome_pending: true}. The founder updates outcome (retained / churned-anyway / partial-retention) in the dashboard after a week — that closed loop is the highest-value support data the studio produces and feeds directly into the third loop (Improve) per ADR-0008. (2) Objection-pattern aggregation → after N=5 cancellation-reason notes, surface a proposal in queue/ to write/update a `support/objection-patterns.md` memory_file enumerating the top 3-5 cancellation reasons and the brain's current best-response pattern for each. This is the support studio's analog to the marketing studio's voice-consistency improvement loop. (3) Decision-stress-testing → if the model cites the same decision (e.g., 'no-discount-under-$X') across 3+ accepted churn-save runs AND the founder later edits the reply to soften the decision-citation, propose a decision-revision in queue/ ('your no-discount-under-$X rule was cited 3x in churn-save and softened 2x — is the threshold still right?'). This is the brain literally noticing when its own decisions are getting stress-tested by real customer behavior — exactly the compounding ADR-0008 calls out as the third-loop deliverable.
safety_constraints: Never offer a discount when brain.recent_decisions contains a 'no-discount' rule, UNLESS the `allowed_offers` input explicitly includes 'discount' for this single run. Never use 'sorry to see you go', 'we'd hate to lose you', 'we appreciate your business', 'we value you as a customer' — all are AI-tells AND retention-marketing register that defeats the founder-personal positioning. Never argue with the cancellation reason even when it's factually wrong about product capability — surface the mismatch gently, do not correct combatively. Never commit to a refund, prorated or otherwise, in the draft body — refunds are founder-side decisions, the draft can include '[I'll process a refund of $X — Oscar to confirm]' as a placeholder. Never promise a feature is on the roadmap unless the brain has a decision or product memory confirming it. Never use exclamation points. Never use 'we' if brain.team has one row — solo-founder voice uses 'I'. Never end with a marketing CTA. The hard 6-sentence ceiling is itself a safety constraint — long replies signal defensiveness and increase legal/commitment-leak surface area.
override_examples: 1. 'For churn-save, always offer 30 days extra access' → add_constraint with {constraint: 'Always include a one-line offer of 30 days extra access on the customer's current plan, regardless of cancellation reason.'}. This is the canonical example from the field definition. 2. 'Never say sorry to see you go' → forbid_pattern with {pattern: 'sorry to see you go'}. 3. 'Always end with 'whatever you decide, the door's open'' → add_constraint with closing-line copy. 4. 'For paying customers (plan=Team or Enterprise), always offer a founder call with a Calendly link' → add_constraint. 5. 'If the customer mentions price, never offer a discount — instead, write 'our price reflects the value we're delivering; happy to walk you through the ROI'' → add_example with the exact phrasing. 6. 'Sign off as 'oc', no last name' → add_constraint. 7. 'For tenure < 30 days, treat as onboarding failure not churn — offer a 30-minute setup call instead of standard offers' → add_constraint (this is a borderline candidate for becoming its own template, 'onboarding-rescue', in v1.x). All seven map cleanly onto the existing J.14/J.15 four-kind override schema; no new override kinds needed.
estimated_loc: ~180 LOC for the prompt builder (churn-save.ts) — slightly heavier than customer-reply (~165 LOC) because of (a) the built-in exemplar few-shot (~20 LOC of hardcoded shape examples), (b) the allowed_offers input parsing and default-menu fallback (~10 LOC), (c) the decisions-required hard-fail logic ('if recent_decisions is empty, surface a warning in the prompt and run with weaker constraints'). The shared support/types.ts helper file (if created) absorbs ~40 LOC of voiceClause/glossaryClause/decisionsClause-for-support-context that's reused across all 5 support templates and is not counted in the per-template LOC. Anchors to the upper end of the 120-200 LOC engineering-template range.
v1_essential: Yes. Churn-save is lower frequency than customer-reply but it's the template that demonstrates the brain's decision-memory pays off (the 'we don't discount' rule is the canonical example in the outline.yaml description, and it only matters for high-stakes templates like this one). Shipping the support studio without churn-save means the studio's pitch is 'AI-Drafts but OSS', which is undifferentiated. With churn-save, the pitch is 'AI-Drafts grounded in your decisions, where the AI knows what you've already ruled out'. That's the BBC differentiation. Recommended ship order: customer-reply (proves voice grounding) → churn-save (proves decision grounding) → bug-ack / incident-status / feature-request-triage (consolidate the pattern).
